These provisions are important for our communities.
We have recently submitted to the, uh,
people's Select Committee on Pay Equity.
What we would, what we found in our, in our research is
that Asian women get, are disproportionately affected
by the ethnic gender wage gap.
Uh, it is one of the few ethnic gender wage
gaps that are actually increasing.
Uh, currently the gender wage gap sits at 14.6% on average,
7.6% for women, but up to 24.2% for Asian women,
and 33% in, um, different in various sectors,
including the academic health sector.
We think that these statistics are very shocking,
and the public service should be taking the lead in
addressing pay equity.
It has flow on effects by viewing, um,
increasing things like representation,
trust in the public service, and it,
and it supports mutually supports the points that, um,
sine has made about diversity and inclusion more generally.
It is an open secret
that the public service doesn't reflect, um,
and doesn't represent all of our communities at present.
It, um, doesn't do very well in terms
of representation at the leadership level.
And things like pay equity are some of the barriers.
It's not a perfect solution,
but we think that just on fairness, public, uh,
or just on fairness, pay equity is important,
and more importantly, it disproportionately affects, uh,
w in our community.
And so we think that the removal of references
to pay equity, particularly in the good employer, uh,
provisions, are deeply concerning.
And we urge this committee not to recommend, um,
these provisions, uh, to continue.
We also wanna make a short comment
on the purpose of the bill.
Interestingly, the current purpose, uh,
already states the importance
of the long term public interest to be considered,
and also that it is on the government to support
and facilitate active citizenship.
Find it quite curious that the amended purpose
does not make reference to these, um,
two points in particular, long term public, um,
interest in active citizenship as well as a, a turn instead
to a focus on, quote unquote meet the
needs of New Zealanders.
At present, we think that the long term view
that the current purpose states is in the benefit
of Asian communities,
and particularly the focus on active ci, active citizenship.
The long-term view is important
because we know that AL is changing,
it's changing demographically.
There are, uh, changes on the horizon, um,
that the public interest needs to consider
and to take into account.
And we think that this purpose in which it frames the whole
act is something that, that, um, shouldn't be removed.
We also know that active citizenship is particularly
important, uh, for Asian communities.
We've just recently made, uh, our own submission on the, um,
electoral amendment bill, and we have, we made concern.
We, we set out our concerns there about
how Asian communities have been historically and con
and contemporarily, um, excluded,
or that there are barriers
to engaging in the democratic process, active citizenship,
and facilitating, facilitating that should be part of
what the public service does.
We think that there are long term benefits to this, such
as strengthening democracy in a time like 2025 when we see
that trust in democratic institutions are rolling
backwards globally.
When we step back, all these three, three points
that we've highlighted, inclusion and diversity, pay equity,
and more broadly in terms of the purpose, uh, we don't think
that that reflects what the public service
should be heading towards.
And in fact, it doesn't set up alor well to, um,
address future concerns.
Before we continue and,
and wrap up, I just wanted to turn over to the committee
and to engage any questions
that, uh, the committee has for us.
Yeah, I'll start with, uh, Francisco Hernandez.
Uh, thank you so much for that chair,
and thank you for your submission, uh, Asian legal Network.
Um, can you elaborate on how having, um,
DEI could actually enhance merit based appointments rather
than going against it?
Uh, yeah. So for example, uh, say for example in,
we'll take counsel, so real local, uh, example.
So we had a park, um, that was up for consultation,
and the people who were there with the clipboards weren't,
uh, were just standing there not able to actually engage
with the people who were utilizing the park, who, you know,
maybe from a different, um, ethnicity
and simply didn't understand that there are different ways
that you have to go up to them.
You can't expect people to go to you.
And until I pulled them by the scruff of their shirts
and said, please talk to the people
who are utilizing this park, that consultation
and the outcomes, the genuine outcomes
of consultation would've never happened.
So it just shows that when it comes to diversity,
engagement in community, um,
and engagement of public services, that's
where DEI comes in.
Uh, we've just got sort of two minutes left.
Um, did you wanna speak more about the long-term, um,
vision, or do you want a question on that now?
Uh, any questions? Well, I'll welcome on that.
Yeah. If you had any questions.
Yeah. So I, I'm just interested in the, um,
you're not the only submitter that's talked about the long
term focus.
I'm, I'm just, you know, wondering how it compares, say,
if we look at the Australian legislation, it doesn't mention
that it's just kind of, um, I, I guess assume
that everyone's takes more than an immediate focus.
But, um, do you have other countries
where you think this maybe that we could compare it to
where it works really well?
We don't have that with us specifically in terms
of other comparable jurisdictions
that have specifically talked about the long-term interest
in the public sector, um, analysis.
However, signaling at a, at a proposed level,
we think is always important.
Um, it's not so much that it's, it's, um,
not present, but it's actually being removed.
That is the issue. And
what the signals when we're interpreting, um,
legislation going forward, the removal
of a focus from the long-term interest
and the replacement, we think that that might create, um,
unintended legal consequences.
And those are things that we need to consider.
We also don't see the justification for its removal,
which is why we're,
we're concerned from our community's point of view.
And probably this is, is this is the key point.
We should be really focused on the long term
because of the challenges that RO faces.
And we think the public service is well positioned
to be explicitly considering the long term.
Um, and it's not just the long term,
but it's long term in relationship
to things like active citizenship.
When we look at the global trends towards backsliding in
democratic participation, that is a call for concern.
So it's that removal in 2025 when we're taking a
look at the global trends.
That is the reason why we're concerned whether it's removal
from, um, a significant piece of legislation like this.
Thank you. Um, and that does actually,
Stanley, bring us to the end of our time.
We have our next submitter in the room.
Thank you for joining us today.
We are going to move now to the clerk of the house, uh,
or the office of the clerk, um, clerk,
the house representing them.
Uh, we've got 10 minutes set aside for your submission.
You might like to leave some time for
questions. Uh, the floor is
Yours. Okay. Thanks Mr.
Chairman and good morning members.
So the submission is a fairly brief one
and really just focuses on one issue, um, around Clause 25,
uh, sorry, clause 45 of the Public Service Amendment bill.
Um, so which is, which deals with the way
that long-term insights
briefings will be prepared in the future.
So under the Public Service Act,
now all departmental chief executives are required
to prepare long-term insights briefings.
Um, and there's been one round of those that's enabled, um,
you know, parliament to engage quite deeply on longer term
thinking, which is something that, um, has been felt
to be absent in the past.
Um, the, and this committee plays a coordinating role in
the, um, handling of long term insights briefings
when they're presented to the house.
At the moment, it's the Governance
and Administration Committee that allocates them
to subject select committees in the last round,
which is the only one we've had.
Um, all committees bar one received one of those briefings.
The commentary on them was, you know, they're
of mixed quality and, and usefulness,
but overall, I think this committee,
and it's in the last Parliament, felt they were useful, um,
and did think there should probably be some more
coordination and, um, I guess evening out of,
of their quality and coverage.
The, uh, the bill that's in front of the committee proposes
that only the Department of Prime Minister
and Cabinet will prepare a long-term
insight briefing in the future.
Um, and I raise that
because it def directly affects parliament's ability
to scrutinize the performance of the government
and of public service departments.
Mm-hmm. Um, at the moment, uh, there's one
of these briefings per department,
they're allocated to committees.
As I said, almost all committees get one,
there's quite good alignment between, um,
subject select committees, subject areas,
and, um, government departments, areas of responsibility.
And that's an intentional feature of the way
that parliamentary committees have been established.
So I guess the concern is that it reduces the ability
of committees as a whole to carry out some of
that long-term scrutiny and
to scrutinize government performance.
Um, but on the other hand, I, I do take the point from the,
uh, the briefing papers
and the regulatory impact statement on this around the need
for better coordination and,
and leadership of it by the DPMC.
And so what, uh, I'm suggesting in the submission is
that we try and preserve the parliamentary scrutiny
by requiring an overarching report from DPMC,
retain the obligation for departmental chief executives
to prepare them for their own subject areas.
But, um, em empower the DPMC
to coordinate the process better.
This would maintain the accountability focus, the sort
of future or the need for future focus in departments
and maintain parliament's ability to,
to scrutinize the government and hold it to account.
So that's, that's the only thing
that the submission's on is just that amendment to Clause 45
to preserve some of that long-term focus that exists now.
And if there are any questions, I'm happy to take them.
Thank you. I'll look,
I'll just ask a really honest question
'cause I, um, I mean, it's, it's interesting
to me, like it almost feels like you are making a,
a policy recommendation in terms of how this could be run.
And, and I just, I I, I've noticed that, you know, the, the,
the officer of the clerks,
some has submitted on a few bills.
Mm. At the same time, we are being told in terms of,
you know, and this one particularly around scrutiny,
um, as it's set out.
At the same time, we're being told
that committees can't be supported
with more fulsome reports when they actually conduct
scrutiny in terms of annual reviews and estimates
because of time.
And yet, I I I guess some of us sit here
and go, well, the, the office has got time to
make commentary on, on bills and policy recommendations.
Yep. Could that not be dedicated to the, you know,
to helping actual scrutiny?
Okay. Well, uh, I'd say,
I guess there's two things to say about that.
That the clerk of the house has always commented on
bills that affect Parliament.
Um, and I think the amount of resource that takes is a,
a pretty small fraction of the amount that's needed
to support select committees.
So it would be effectively the time that I
and part of one staff member could give
to support committee scrutiny.
I think there is a, a balance to be achieved.
I certainly would like to be able
to provide greater support to committees.
And I hope that with the passage of the Parliament Bill,
we'll be able to, um,
but I think we'd be missing an opportunity if, um,
I didn't carry up my role, um, in, in commenting on,
on legislation that affects parliament.
And, and I understand that.
I guess I'm, I'm, I'm, I'm, I'm thinking out loud here,
but, um, I understand that where it comes
to maybe the actual workings of parliament, but,
but this is more, feels like that, you know,
a policy decision, which up until 2020 didn't happen
and we seem to cope, okay, uh, they've only been run once.
There's a policy decision taken by the government of the day
that says, actually we are not gonna proceed with them.
A as you said yourself, uh, they were pretty mixed reviews.
Um, and,
and we, there's a, we think maybe
the government suggests there's better things they
could focus their efforts on.
Uh, then just the, yeah, creating multiple reports.
I, I think the question is more, we,
we are getting into sort of a policy decision rather than a
working of parliament.
Um, well, I think it's a policy decision
that affects the working of parliament.
It's, it's meaning that something
that committees currently can work on,
they won't be able to in the future.
So I do stay away from government policy
that doesn't affect parliament.
I only co think I comment only on things that do,
and I really see that this does,
because it does remove one
of the work streams from committees.
But I mean, doesn't every policy decision impact the work
that comes to committees?
But this one is taking away a specific piece
of work from committees.
Yes. All, I mean, all, all policy that is enacted
through legislation affects
some committee in some way or another.
They have to look at the bill as you are doing now.
Yeah, yeah. Yeah. Um, I think that's true,
but we are, a policy means
that committees will no longer be able
to do something they can currently do.
That's why I felt it was necessary to highlight
It. But does this,
and I guess as I sort of think
through this issue, does this actually stop the committee
having a long-term view in terms
of local government for this committee?
Perhaps, uh, when we conduct scrutiny, um,
when we're looking at briefings
or inquiries when we have departments
before us, um, nothing says that it can't happen.
It's just going to be one centralized report coordinator
by DPMC rather than every chief executive being made
accountable for one.
Um, yes, that's right. Does
that actually stop the committees doing what they did prior
to 2020 when these didn't come in this form?
Uh, it, it doesn't, I, I think what happened prior
to 2020 was, I guess a feeling there wasn't
that much long-term focus.
Um, I, I think that's what led to these,
although it wasn't involved in the policy development
because it's a, as you know, a government policy.
Um, nothing stops committees looking
at anything they want to.
All I'm highlighting is that, um, a report that would come
to each committee that actually focuses on those areas in
their subject area will no longer be available.
And I, I think there's a way of achieving
what the government wants to,
but also something that's still useful for parliament.
So a bit more sort of spoon fed.
Uh, well only to the extent that all advice to committees,
if you sort of think of that as spoon feeding, I suppose
that, you know, that is a way of looking at it,
but you are all busy people
with a lot of things to deal with.
I would've thought getting advice that was specific
to it was more useful, um,
but ultimately as a decision you'll have to make.
Yeah. Um, Francisco Hernandez, uh,
Thank you so much, uh, chair
and thank you for, um, giving your viewpoint on this.
I think in terms of reducing, um,
I think the primary objection you have is that the, um,
that the committees will no longer get the specific reports
that are produced by the department
and that that will have the impact
of somewhat slightly reducing parliamentary scrutiny.
Yes. And you see the way that
that could be addressed is through better coordination
through the DPMC, perhaps,
and maybe a little bit more focused targeting
of the reports in some way.
Yeah, I, I think, I mean, there's certainly, you know, I,
I think the, the feedback on those
reports was that they were not perfect.
They were a work in progress
and that first round had variable results,
but overall was better than not having them.
Mm-hmm. Um, so I do think there's sort
of a middle ground trying to achieve
what the government wants to in better coordination of it.
But I, I still think there is a useful role
for departments in feeding into committees, uh,
to individual subject committees
on it through this committee.
Thank you. Thank you for your time this morning.
We are going to suspend when we are back at one o'clock,
we'll be as the whole committee down the corridor
and then we'll return to subcommittee later on.
Thank you. Thank you.

source_metadata.description:
Wednesday, 15th October 2025 | Governance and Administration Subcommittee B

Public Service Amendment Bill

Hearing of evidence:

National Delegate Team at Department of Conservation, Public Service Association
Paula Warren, PSA National Delegate

Policy Hub - Victoria University of Wellington
Andrew Jackson, Operational Manager

Dr. Malcolm Menzies

Parents of Vision Impaired NZ
Dr Rebekah Graham, National Executive Officer

Asian Legal Network
Darryn Ooi, Co-lead advocacy

Office of the Clerk
Dr. David Wilson, Clerk of the House of Representatives
